chunk_summary: Guidance on iron diet counseling for preventing deficiency, focusing on milk limits, iron-rich foods, enhancers like vitamin C, and reducing inhibitors like tea/coffee. Includes numeric milk intake guidelines and practical dietary substitutions for Indian toddlers and infants.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of milk limits in preventing iron deficiency in toddlers and infants.
Identify iron-rich foods and enhancers suitable for vegetarian and non-vegetarian diets.
Learn strategies to reduce dietary inhibitors of iron absorption.
Incorporate follow-up and infection control into dietary counseling.
key_facts: Milk intake for 1–5 year olds should be 400–600 mL/day; >800 mL/day is a red flag for iron deficiency.
Introduce iron-rich complementary foods at 6 months, such as egg yolk, meat purees, and iron-fortified cereals.
Pair plant-based iron sources (legumes, beans, soy) with vitamin C–rich foods like citrus, guava, and tomato to enhance absorption.
Avoid giving iron supplements with milk and limit tea/coffee with meals in older children.
Separate iron and calcium supplements by several hours to prevent absorption interference.
reference_quote: Aim for about 400–600 mL/day of milk or equivalent dairy in 1–5 year olds; intakes >800 mL/day are a red flag for iron deficiency.
document_key_topics: Milk limits and iron deficiency prevention
Enhancers and inhibitors of iron absorption
